text: we are seeing a case of laparoscopic ripples procedure for a case of uh sixty five years old male with obstructive jaundice diagnosed to a peri-amplary carcinoma uh cbd was uh uh twelve mm and mpd was eight mm bilirubin of eleven no accessory arteries but right hepatic artery passing anterior to cbd this was the four positions with camera placed in the head and surgeon standing in between the legs

The surgeon begins the procedure by dissecting the gastrocolic ligament in a thin patient, carefully identifying the proper plane to avoid entering the mesocolon. The right gastroepiploic artery is visualized branching from the gastroduodenal artery (GDA) and is taken down, followed by the identification and management of the right gastroepiploic vein.

The surgeon traces the gastrocolic loop of Henle, formed by the right gastrocolic vein, accessory glycolic vein, and ASPDV. The accessory glycolic vein is taken down, and the gastrocolic trunk entering the SMV is clipped. The right gastroepiploic vein is also taken down, and the gastrocolic vein is divided, revealing the SMV and creating a partial tunnel behind the neck of the pancreas.

The surgeon performs Kocherization by pulling the second part of the duodenum with assistance, entering proper anatomical planes. The hepatic flexure may be mobilized if necessary. Once the inferior vena cava (IVC) is exposed, the attachments of the third part of the duodenum are taken down.

The surgeon completes extended caucarization and releases the DJ flexure. Using a 60 mm stapler, the jejunum is divided 15 centimeters from the DJ flexure. The mesentery is dissected down to the DJ flexure and repositioned into the supracolic compartment. The duodenum is reflected caudally and cranially, revealing the inferior pancreaticoduodenal vessels branching from the SMA and SMV. The inferior pancreaticoduodenal vein is clipped and cut, allowing visualization of the SMA. Dissection proceeds slowly, close to the vessel.

The surgeon divides the inferior pancreaticoduodenal veins, which may vary in number, and reflects the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) to reveal the superior mesenteric artery (SMA). The inferior pancreaticoduodenal artery is observed arising from the SMA, and additional pancreaticoduodenal vessels are clipped and divided to minimize bleeding before pancreas transection.

The falciform ligament is lifted using transabdominal sutures. The surgeon then transects the stomach, narrowing the right gastroepiploic arcade and clipping both the artery and vein. A pylorus-resecting pancreatoduodenectomy is performed just proximal to the pylorus. The right gastric artery is taken down from the right hepatic or common hepatic artery. The lesser sac is opened and released proximally to position the stomach for anastomosis. The stomach is transected using a golden 60 mm stapler, completing two major transections: the jejunum and the stomach.

The surgeon performs lymphadenectomy on the lymph nodes located on the common hepatic artery, using a vessel sealing device to roll down the superior border of the pancreas and expose the artery. The common hepatic artery is visible, giving rise to the proper hepatic artery above and the gastroduodenal artery below. The gastroduodenal artery is dissected and clamped to check pulsations, then double clipped, leaving a stump.

The surgeon leaves a one-centimeter stump and completes the division, allowing reflection for lymphadenectomy behind the portal vein. The portal vein is exposed after the division of the gastroduodenal artery, revealing the splenic vein branching into the portal vein. An umbilical tape is looped around the neck of the pancreas and tied proximally. The uncinate process is completely separated from the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) before transecting the neck of the pancreas using monopolar cautery. Bleeding is anticipated at the upper and lower poles during this transection.

The surgeon exposes and sharply divides the duct of the uncinate process without using an energy source, followed by the division of the cephalic part of the pancreas body, addressing any bleeding points. The common bile duct (CBD) is dissected from the portal vein, and the postoperative pancreaticoduodenal vein (PSPDV) is divided. Lymph nodes behind the hepatic ductal ligament (HDL) are removed, revealing the lower border of the liver. The gallbladder is decompressed, and the cystic duct and artery are dissected, with the cystic artery being taken down. The cystic duct, which runs parallel to the CBD, is divided distal to the right hepatic artery to facilitate anastomosis.

The surgical field reveals the portal vein, splenic vein, and superior mesenteric vein (SMV) after sharp division near the confluence. The surgeon identifies the inferior vena cava (IVC), left renal vein, and superior mesenteric artery (SMA) region. The jejunum is brought through the mesocolic window to the right of the middle colic artery, and a pancreaticojejunostomy is performed using the modified Blunger technique due to the large pancreatic duct and firm pancreas.

The surgeon performs suturing on the atrophic pancreas using through-and-through sutures with 2-0 Prolene, taking bites from the seromuscular layer of the jejunum and back through the pancreas. This technique occludes vessels and minor ducts, ensuring a snug fit of the jejunum against the pancreas. Ductomucosa sutures are placed using 4-0 Prolene PDS, starting at the eleven o'clock position and continuing around the duct, with ten interrupted sutures securing the posterior and anterior walls. The pancreas is visibly atrophic, and no stent is used due to the large duct size.

The surgeon places the final two sutures for the ductomucosa anastomosis under direct vision, ensuring no gaps remain. The assistant holds the sutures in position before tying, and superficial sutures are added to secure the anastomosis without penetrating the posterior wall of the main pancreatic duct.

The surgeon takes a proline suture through the anterior wall of the jejunum, rolling it over the anterior part of the pancreas, similar to the posterior placement. Capsular to seromuscular bites are taken on the anterior jejunal wall at the duct site, ensuring snug coverage of the transected pancreatic surface. A through-and-through suture is placed caudally, securing the jejunum around the pancreas. The modified Blunger technique pancreaticojejunostomy is completed. The surgeon then proceeds to hepaticojejunostomy, using an epigastric port to retract the left liver lobe. An intratomy is performed, and continuous V-lock sutures are placed in the wide duct and jejunum, starting at the right corner and progressing to the left corner.

The surgeon completes the posterior layer of sutures between the common bile duct (CBD) and the jejunum using a V-lock suture. The anterior layer is then sutured with eight to ten bites, ensuring careful retraction to avoid the posterior wall of either structure. The V-lock suture is pulled snugly, and a small clip is applied for safety. The surgical field is checked for bile leakage, which is absent.

The gallbladder is removed, and surgical drains are placed near the hepatico-jejunostomy and pancreatic jejunostomy sites. A loop of jejunum is selected and tied to the stomach, followed by creating a small incision at the umbilicus for gastrojejunostomy and feeding jejunostomy.
